metadata.summary: A structured process to divide a market into distinct groups using two to three differentiating dimensions, score each against weighted attractiveness criteria, and select at most one primary target.

content.title: Audience Segmentation
content.summary: A structured process to divide a potential market into distinct, mutually exclusive groups using two to three differentiating dimensions (demographic, behavioural, psychographic, or needs-based), score each segment against six weighted attractiveness criteria, and select at most one primary target for pre-traction products. Segments are maintained as YAML/JSON for reproducible quarterly re-scoring.

why.p: "Everyone is our customer" erodes paid-acquisition efficiency and message resonance. Segmentation makes targeting trade-offs explicit and falsifiable: by scoring Size, Growth, Reachability, Profitability, Fit, and Competition with fixed weights, teams can defend their choice of primary segment without relying on intuition. Demographic-only segmentation is the most common failure mode — it yields lookalikes that do not predict purchase.

list.item: Pre-launch positioning when the team is debating which buyer to target first.
Post-launch when "everyone is our customer" is eroding paid-acquisition efficiency.
Pricing-tier design that needs distinct buying behaviours and willingness-to-pay levels.
Pivoting into a new market where the prior segmentation no longer maps.
Product-tier or product-line decisions (free/pro/enterprise; consumer/SMB/mid-market).
Pre-traction MVPs with fewer than 50 customers — use one persona, not segment matrices.
Strict niche businesses where the entire ICP fits one segment by design.
Account-based sales motions where named accounts replace segmentation.
Ephemeral campaigns — A/B-test creatives instead.

section.name: segmentation-types
section.p: Use at most two or three dimensions per segmentation; denser matrices are unreadable in stakeholder conversations. Combine quantitative (firmographics, behavioural) and qualitative (needs, attitudes) dimensions — pure quant misses messaging insights.

list.item: Demographic: age, income, occupation, company size, industry. Best for B2B targeting and pricing tiers.
Behavioural: usage frequency, purchase history, feature usage, engagement level, buying stage. Best for retention and product development.
Psychographic: values, attitudes, lifestyle, motivations. Best for messaging and brand positioning.
Needs-based: problem severity, current solution, budget, speed priority, support needs. Best for product tiers and pricing strategy.

rule.statement: Gather data from CRM, analytics, survey responses, and sales conversations; choose two to three dimensions that maximise inter-segment variance and minimise intra-segment variance.
rule.rationale: Dimensions chosen without variance analysis produce segments that look different but behave the same — they do not guide distinct go-to-market or product decisions.

rule.statement: Validate segment size against an addressable population (LinkedIn audience estimate, paid-ads reach, list-rental quote); theoretical size means nothing without addressability evidence.
rule.rationale: Segments that are not addressable via any channel are worthless regardless of their size on paper.

rule.statement: Ensure mutually exclusive segments: every customer should fit one and only one segment after rules apply. Orphans signal definition gaps.
rule.rationale: Overlapping segments prevent attribution and make channel and pricing decisions ambiguous.

rule.statement: Always include a do-not-target segment with explicit reasoning (low LTV, high CAC, support-cost-heavy). Pair the top segment with one channel and one message.
rule.rationale: Without an explicit do-not-target definition, teams waste acquisition budget on low-ROI users.

section.name: attractiveness-scoring-rubric
section.p: Score each segment 1-5 on six criteria; apply fixed weights to produce a weighted total. Re-score quarterly using the same rubric weights; weight changes must be flagged as a methodology change.

list.item: Size (20%): how many people or companies are in this segment?
Growth (15%): is the segment growing as a share of the market?
Reachability (20%): can we access them economically via existing channels?
Profitability (20%): will they pay enough to sustain the business model?
Fit (15%): do our strengths match this segment's needs?
Competition (10%): how crowded is this segment currently?

rule.statement: Flag any score with no evidence; do not accept agent-generated segment sizes ("estimated 200K SMBs in NYC") without a cited source.
rule.rationale: LLMs invent segment sizes that are decorative; unsupported numbers give false precision to strategic decisions.

section.name: worked-crm-tool-example
section.p: CRM tool segmentation: dimensions = team size x sales complexity. Decision: focus on Agency segment.
section.code:
Dimensions: Team size (Solo, 2-5, 6-20, 20+) x Sales complexity (Transactional, Consultative)
| Segment | Profile | Primary Need | WTP | Weighted Score | |-------------|-----------------------|----------------------|---------|----------------| | Freelancer | Solo x Transactional | Simple pipeline | $9/mo | 2.8 | | Agency | 2-5 x Consultative | Deal tracking | $29/mo | 4.1 | | Startup | 6-20 x Transactional | Automation, reporting| $49/mo | 3.5 | | Enterprise | 20+ x Consultative | Custom, integrations | Custom | 2.2 (reach) |
Decision: Focus on Agency — highest weighted score; high WTP, underserved, reachable via design communities. Do-not-target: Enterprise (reachability = 1; sales cycle exceeds runway).

section.name: worked-online-course-example
section.p: Online course segmentation: dimensions = learning goal x time available.
section.code:
Dimensions: Goal (Career change, Skill upgrade, Hobby) x Time (<5h/wk, 5-10h, 10+h)
| Segment | Profile | Key Trait | WTP | |------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|--------| | Career Changers | Career change x 10+h | Committed, outcome-focused | $400+ | | Upskilling Pros | Upgrade x 5-10h | Busy, pays for time savings | $150+ | | Hobbyists | Hobby x <5h | Fun-focused, price-sensitive| $30 |
Decision: Focus on Upskilling Pros — pays for time savings, reachable via LinkedIn and newsletters.

antipattern.description: Producing eight micro-segments using five dimensions without a cap.
antipattern.reason: Segments beyond four become unmanageable; force a two to three dimension cap and merge the long tail into a do-not-target group.

antipattern.description: Demographic-only segmentation (age, gender, location) for a B2B product.
antipattern.reason: Demographics predict lookup, not purchase; B2B requires industry, employee count, and role as primary axes, with needs-based dimensions second.

antipattern.description: Behavioural-clustering agent over-fits to one outlier whale customer, creating a segment of one.
antipattern.reason: Require feature standardisation and scree-plot evidence for k before accepting cluster results.

antipattern.description: Reverse-ETL bot syncing segments to ad platforms without an approval gate.
antipattern.reason: Agents merging segments to paid media without a kill switch can exhaust ad budgets in hours; require human approval before production activation.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use agents to ingest CRM, analytics, and survey data; propose candidate segmentation dimensions; score segments against attractiveness criteria from this methodology; emit profile cards. Decisions about which segment to pursue belong to humans (commercial trade-offs). Agents should never propose a target without addressability evidence and a sizing range. Maintain segments as a structured artefact (YAML/JSON) so re-scoring is reproducible quarter-over-quarter.
Recommended subagents:

list.item: faion-persona-builder-agent — produces persona-grade detail per chosen segment.
A dimension-finder subagent: runs feature-importance / variance analysis on CRM/analytics features to surface the two most differentiating axes.
brainstorm — diverges on candidate segment names + needs framings, converges on at most four mutually exclusive segments.
A scorer subagent: applies the weighted criteria (Size 20, Growth 15, Reachability 20, Profitability 20, Fit 15, Competition 10) consistently across segments.
improver — quarterly refresh against fresh data.

section.p: Prompt patterns:
section.code: Given the attached CRM export (`crm.csv`) with fields `[plan, mrr, signup_source, employee_count, industry, churn_30, feature_use_score]`, propose two candidate dimensions that maximise inter-segment variance and minimise intra-segment variance. Show a confusion matrix for each. Output as `dimensions.json`.
Given segments `[A, B, C]` and the attractiveness rubric below, score each 1-5 per criterion with one-line justification. Compute weighted total. Flag any score with no evidence.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: CLI tools for segmentation analysis and activation:

list.item: dbt — Cohort and segment SQL pipelines. Docs: https://docs.getdbt.com
Posthog / Amplitude / Mixpanel APIs — Behavioural-segment cohorts. Docs: https://posthog.com / https://amplitude.com/docs/apis
HubSpot / Salesforce CLI — CRM segment ops. Docs: https://developers.hubspot.com / https://developer.salesforce.com
Segment / RudderStack — CDP routing per segment. Docs: https://segment.com / https://www.rudderstack.com
pandas + scikit-learn — k-means / hierarchical clustering for segmentation. Install: pip install pandas scikit-learn
kmodes — Categorical-variable clustering. Install: pip install kmodes
Notion / Airtable API — Segment library. Docs: https://developers.notion.com
Census / Hightouch — Reverse-ETL segments to ad/email tools. Docs: https://www.getcensus.com / https://hightouch.com
Crayon / Klue (if exposed) — Competitive intel per segment. Docs: https://www.crayon.co / https://klue.com

section.p: Services and applications:

list.item: Segment / RudderStack (SaaS/OSS) — CDP for segment-driven activation. Agent-friendly via REST + SDK.
Census / Hightouch (SaaS) — Reverse-ETL to ad platforms. Agent-friendly via REST.
HubSpot / Salesforce (SaaS) — CRM as segment ground truth. Agent-friendly via REST.
Posthog / Amplitude / Mixpanel (SaaS) — Behavioural data. Agent-friendly via REST.
Apollo.io / ZoomInfo (SaaS) — B2B firmographic enrichment. Agent-friendly via REST.
Clearbit (now HubSpot) (SaaS) — Firmographic data on sign-ups. Agent-friendly via REST.
SurveyMonkey / Typeform (SaaS) — Psychographic survey delivery. Agent-friendly via REST.
Forsta / Qualtrics (SaaS) — Enterprise segmentation studies. Agent-friendly via REST.
Productboard (SaaS) — Segment ↔ feature roadmap link. Agent-friendly via REST.
Crayon / Klue (SaaS) — Competitive density per segment. Agent-friendly via REST.
Pick two dimensions, not five — anything denser is unreadable in stakeholder conversations.
Combine quantitative (firmographics, behavioural) and qualitative (needs, attitudes); pure quant misses messaging insights.
Validate segment size against an addressable population (LinkedIn audience, paid-ads reach, list rentals); theoretical size means nothing.
Always include a "do-not-target" segment with explicit reasoning (low LTV, high CAC, support-cost-heavy).
Pair the top segment with one channel and one message; if you can't pick one, the segment is too broad.
Re-score quarterly using the same rubric weights; weight changes must be flagged as a methodology change.
Sanity-check exclusivity: every customer should fit one and only one segment after rules apply; orphans signal definition gaps.
Map segments to existing personas explicitly; the two artefacts must agree.

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: Segment definitions decay; behavioural segments especially shift in 6-12 months as products and competitors evolve.
2x2 matrices give clean visuals but oversimplify multi-dimensional reality; don't over-trust them.
Demographic-only segmentation is the most common failure mode — yields lookalikes that don't predict purchase.
Segments that are not addressable (no channel reaches them economically) are worthless regardless of theoretical size.
Mutually exclusive segmentation in B2B is hard: a contact may be both "decision maker" and "user".
Agents asked to "find segments" without constraints often output 8 micro-segments by demographics; force a 2-3 dimension cap.
LLMs invent segment sizes ("estimated 200K SMBs in NYC") that are decorative; demand a source for any quantitative claim.
Behavioural-clustering agents over-fit to noisy features (e.g., one outlier whale); require feature standardisation and scree-plot evidence for k.
B2B segmentation generated from B2C tropes (lifestyle, age) misses fit/firmographics; force industry, employee_count, role as priors.
Reverse-ETL bots syncing segments to ad tools without a kill switch can blow ad budgets; require approval gating in production.
Agents merging "current solution" responses across surveys conflate competitors with substitutes; clarify in the prompt.
When proposing the final target, agents tend to pick the largest segment; force a weighted-score table so reachability and fit aren't ignored.

section.name: k-means-clustering-script
section.p: Inline behavioural-segment k-means (deterministic seed for reproducibility):
section.code:
# segment.py — k-means on usage features, emits segments.json import json, pandas as pd from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
df = pd.read_csv("usage.csv") # cols: customer_id, mrr, dau_w, feat_score, seats features = ["mrr", "dau_w", "feat_score", "seats"] X = StandardScaler().fit_transform(df[features])
km = KMeans(n_clusters=4, n_init=20, random_state=42).fit(X) df["segment"] = km.labels_
profile = ( df.groupby("segment")[features] .agg(["mean", "median", "count"]) .round(2) .to_dict() ) out = { "n_segments": 4, "feature_importance": dict(zip(features, km.cluster_centers_.var(axis=0).tolist())), "profile": profile, } with open("segments.json", "w") as f: json.dump(out, f, indent=2)

template.purpose: Full segmentation analysis doc with scoring table and target strategy.
Single-segment profile card for stakeholder communication.
k-means clustering on usage features, emits segments.json.
